title: Czech vowels, length, and stress
summary: Build a reliable pronunciation foundation: five basic vowel qualities, short–long contrast, common vowel pairs, and first-syllable stress.
objectives: Read a, e, i/y, o, and u consistently
Recognize long vowels marked with čárka or kroužek
Place stress on the first syllable

sections.heading: Five basic vowel qualities
sections.body: Czech spelling is much more predictable than English spelling. The letters i and y represent the same vowel sound, although they affect surrounding consonants differently in later lessons.

sections.heading: Short and long vowels
sections.body: Vowel length is meaningful. Long vowels are normally written with an acute accent; ů is another spelling of long u. Hold a long vowel roughly twice as long as the corresponding short vowel.

sections.heading: Vowel pairs and stress
sections.body: Common written pairs combine predictably. Czech word stress normally falls on the first syllable, even when a word is long.

resources.title: Original lesson: Czech Vowels
resources.note: Includes the original examples and image attributions.

exercises.prompt: Which word contains a long vowel?
exercises.explanation: The acute accent in ý marks a long vowel.

exercises.prompt: Type the Czech word for tea.
Where does normal Czech word stress fall?
exercises.explanation: Czech normally stresses the first syllable.

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the word you hear.
exercises.explanation: The voice says být with a long ý.

exercises.prompt: Listen and type the Czech word.
